content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMrvnssloN
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
March 4, 1998
Nathan John Rangel
President, Adventure Connection
Post Office Box 475
•
Coloma, California 95613
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-032
Dear Mr. Rangel:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
Please keep in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct that has already taken place. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 75.) In addition, the
Commission does not act as finder of fact when issuing advice. Our advice is based upon the
facts presented in your request.
QUESTIONS
1. Do you have a conflict of interest if you actively participate in the management plan
public input process held by the Department of Parks and Recreation related to the Mammoth
Bar Off-Highway Vehicle Park?
2. Do you have a conflict of interest if the California State Parks and Recreation
Commission holds public hearings and votes on a decision regarding the Mammoth Bar Off-
Highway Vehicle Park?
CONCLUSION
1 and 2. Based upon the facts provided, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision
concerning the expansion of the Mammoth Bar Off-Highway Vehicle Park will have a material
financial effect on your businesses.
Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-032
Page 2
FACTS
You are currently an appointed member of the California State Parks and Recreation
Commission. You also own and operate three separate businesses--a professional whitewater
rafting company, Adventure Connection, Inc.; a general store, The Lotus Store; and a film and
television production support business, Adventure Production Services.
You are also an officer of a trade association, California Outdoors, which consists of
professional outfitters throughout California. In your capacity as the president ofthat
organization, you lobby on behalf of the organization’s members. You do not receive
compensation or gifts of any kind for your service.
The Department ofParks and Recreation (“State Parks”) permits your rafting company to
operate in the Auburn State Recreation Area on the Middle and North Forks of the American
River. The issue that you are concerned with involves the Middle Fork ofthe American River.
That river begins at Owbow reservoir and the whitewater section runs 17 miles downstream to an
area called Greenwood Bridge where your company terminates its trips. The river continues
downstream for nine more miles to Mammoth Bar where there is currently a state park,
Mammoth Bar Off-Highway Vehicle Park.
Your rafting company does not operate on that final nine mile section, nor does the
company advertise it because it has only Class I and II rapids, not whitewater rapids. Your
rafting company has not used that section ofriver in over ten years. Your general store is located
about 15 miles from the Mammoth Bar park in Lotus. Commercial establishments that service
the Mammoth Bar park area are located in Auburn and Cool, which are closer to the park than
Lotus. Your production company has never and does not intend to use the Mammoth Bar park
area because there is no road access in that area. In the past, your production company has
operated 15-16 miles upstream from the Mammoth Bar park and five to six miles downstream
from the park.
State Parks is currently taking public input on a management plan for the Mammoth Bar
Off-Highway Vehicle Park. It is considering a number of alternatives for that facility including
the possibility of doubling its physical area. Such a move could cause park activity to extend an
additional three miles upstream. You believe that such a scenario would not affect your
businesses in any way because your businesses do not come into any contact with the Mammoth
Bar park.
California Outdoors wants you to lobby State Parks regarding the extension ofthe
Mammoth Bar park. The trade association is more concerned about the environmental impact of
the park expansion rather than the fiscal impact because most of its members never utilize that
stretch of the river. State Parks’ work on the management plan may come before the State Park
and Recreation Commission if the management plan is not consistent with the Auburn State
Recreation Area General Plan.


File No. A-98-032
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or on any of the following:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand ($1,000) or more.
***
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred and fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.” (Section 87103(a),(c),(d).)
Pursuant to section 87 103(c), your rafting company, general store, and production
companies are presumably sources of income to you of $250 or more within the preceding 12
months. In addition, you have an investment interest in your businesses that may be worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87 103(a).) As the owner, you have a financial interest in your
businesses under section 87103(d).
It does not appear that you have a financial interest in the
trade organization because you do not receive income from the organization.
Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making, or in any way use your official
position to influence a governmental decision if that decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material fmancial effect on any ofyour businesses.
Making or Participating in Making a Governmental Decision
A public official “makes a governmental decision” when the official, acting within the
authority ofhis or her office or position, votes on a matter; appoints a person; obligates his or her
agency to any course of action; enters into any contractual agreement on behalf ofhis or her
agency; or determines not to act unless such determination is made because of his or her financial
interest. (Regulation 18700(b).)


File No. A-98-032
Page 4
A public official “participates in making a governmental decision” when, acting within
the authority ofhis or her position, the official negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental decision; or advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker, either directly or without significant substantive
review. (Regulation 18700(c).)
Making or participating in making a governmental decision does not include the
appearance by a public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course
of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself or herself on matters related
to the official’s personal interests. (Regulation 18700(d).) An official’s “personal interests”
include an interest in real property wholly owned by the official or members ofhis or her
immediate family; a business entity wholly owned by the official or members ofhis or her
immediate family; and a business entity over which the official exercises sole discretion and
control, or over which the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole discretion and
control. (Regulation 18700.1(b)(1).) Since you seek to represent the association’s interests, the
“personal interests” exception does not apply to you.
Thus, ifthe Parks and Recreation Commission takes action on the management plan
involving the Mammoth Bar park, you will be making or participating in making a governmental
decision if you participate in any hearings or possible votes related to the management plan.
Influencing a Governmental Decision
With regard to a governmental decision that is within or before an agency other than the
official’s agency, and other than an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of
the official’s agency, a public official is attempting to his or her official position to “influence a
governmental decision” if the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative
of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency. Such actions
include, but are not limited to the use of official stationery. (Regulation 18700.1(c).)
With regard to a governmental decision that is within or before an official’s agency, the
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts
to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence,
include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client or customer. (Regulation 18700.1(a).)
A governmental decision is within or
before an official’s agency if the decision may come before, is appealable to, or may be
reconsidered by the official’s agency. (Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-94-0 13.)
As previously discussed, an official is not attempting to influence a governmental
decision if the official appears in the same manner as any other member ofthe general public
before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself
or herself on a matter that is related to his or her personal interests. (Regulation 18700.1(b).)


File No. A-98-032
Page 5
Accordingly, you will be influencing a governmental decision if you participate in State
Parks’ management pian public input process and you purport to act as a representative ofthe
Parks and Recreation Conmiission. In addition, you will be influencing a governmental decision
if you contact, appear before, or otherwise attempt to influence any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the Parks and Recreation Commission regarding the Mammoth Bar park.
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial lilcelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Your rafting company utilizes a 17-mile section ofthe American River that ends nine
miles away from the Mammoth Bar park. Your rafting company has not used that section of
river in over ten years. You own a general store in Lotus that is located about 15 miles from the
Mammoth Bar park. Commercial establishments that service the Mammoth Bar park area are
located in Auburn and Cool, which are closer to the park than Lotus. Your production company
has never and does not intend to use the Mammoth Bar park area because there is no road access
to that area. In the past, your production company has operated 15-16 miles upstream from the
Mammoth Bar park and five to six miles downstream from the park. Based upon these facts, it
does not appear that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision concerning the Mammoth Bar
park will have a material financial effect upon your businesses.
Materiality
The foreseeability element of the conflict analysis does not appear to be met. Therefore,
it is not necessary to determine whether the financial effect ofthe decision will be material.
However, ifyou find that it is reasonably foreseeable that your businesses will be financially
affected by a decision involving the Mammoth Bar park, you may not make, participate in
making, or use you official position to influence the decision ifthe financial effect ofthe
decision will be material.
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations that provide guidance concerning
whether the foreseeable effect of a decision is material. These regulations apply different
standards depending on whether the decision will directly or indirectly affect the official’s
economic interest. The effect of a decision is deemed to be material if any person or business
entity that has been a source of income to the official of $250 or more in the preceding 12
months is directly involved in a decision before the official’s agency. (Regulation 1 8702(a)(1).)
A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official’s agency when
that person or entity initiates, is the named party in, or is the subject ofthe proceeding
concerning the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)


File No. A-98-032
Page 6
When an official has an economic interest in a small business entity indirectly involved in
a governmental decision, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is set forth in
Regulation 18702.2(g) (copy enclosed).
Section 87104
In addition to the Act’s general conflict-of-interest provisions, section 87104 prohibits
any public official from acting as an agent or attorney for, or otherwise representing, any other
person for compensation before his or her agency, or officer or employee thereof, ifthe
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing action on a contract, grant,
loan, license, permit, or other entitlement for use. The prohibition in section 87104 applies
where the member will be compensated by some third party. (Geismar Advice Letter, No. A-95-
407.) This section does not appear to apply to your facts. Your facts suggest that you will not
receive any compensation for your efforts related to the Mammoth Bar park.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or on any of the following:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand ($1,000) or more.
